event: Mary Magdalene at the Door of Simon the Pharisee
symbols: Magdalene approaching the door — the moment of decision to humble herself before Christ
elaborate dress — the worldly life she is about to leave behind
deer in the garden — the soul thirsting for God as the deer pants for water
doorway threshold — the passage from sin to repentance, from the world to grace
description: A detailed black and white drawing showing an elaborately dressed woman at the doorway of a grand building. She wears a richly ornamented gown and appears to be stepping toward or pausing at the entrance. Several other figures are visible near the doorway and on steps. A deer stands in a garden to the right. The architecture is ornate with arches and decorative elements. A lush garden with flowers fills the foreground. The overall tone is intricate and narrative.
theological_description: This scene depicts the moment described in Luke 7:36-50, just before Mary Magdalene enters the house of Simon the Pharisee to anoint Jesus’ feet with her tears and costly ointment. The Magdalene stands at the threshold between her former life of sin and the transformative encounter with Christ that will make her the great penitent of Christian tradition. Her elaborate dress represents the worldly life she will renounce. Jesus’ words to Simon—‘Her sins, which are many, are forgiven, for she loved much’ (Luke 7:47)—establish the revolutionary principle that the depth of love is proportional to the awareness of forgiveness. The deer in the garden evokes Psalm 42:1: ‘As the deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God’—the Magdalene’s soul thirsts for the living God she is about to encounter. This moment of approach to Christ’s presence becomes the archetype of conversion.

scripture_refs.reason: The sinful woman entering Simon’s house to anoint Jesus’ feet with tears and ointment
Her sins, which are many, are forgiven, for she loved much
The deer in the garden evokes the soul thirsting for God as the deer pants for water
The anointing at Bethany, connected to the tradition of Mary’s anointing
